quotes.verbatim_quote: use other notes in the bass than the root note.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the core definition of a chord inversion as used throughout the chapter — placing a chord tone other than the root in the bass voice.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Notice that for each group of chords, the root is in the bass for the first chord shape,
then the 3rd, 5th, and the 7th after that.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This establishes the systematic ordering of the four inversions presented in every chord-type group, giving the student the navigational logic for all the diagrams that follow.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Each of these chord inversions can be applied to that chord in your comping. For
example, if you see a Cmaj7 chord symbol you can play any inversion of Cmaj7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the practical performance rule: any inversion is a legitimate substitute for a chord symbol, which is the foundational comping technique the chapter teaches.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Just know that each inversion will have a slightly different sound and some voicings will
work better than others.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the student the evaluative rule — inversions are not interchangeable in all contexts, and ear-based selection is required.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Though they use the same notes, putting the root or the 3rd in the bass causes the
chord to have a slightly different flavor.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explains the acoustic principle behind why inversions matter — same pitch content, different hierarchical emphasis produces a perceptible timbral/harmonic difference.
